metric_name: Snap Appliance market rank — NAS volume
metric_value: Number one, volume leading provider of NAS solutions from workgroup to enterprise
notes: Self-reported but consistent with installed base data.

metric_value: Over 130,000 NAS units installed as of October 2003
notes: Significant installed base for a company that had just restarted as private in 2002.

metric_value: First Snap Servers launched by Meridian Data in 1998; Quantum acquired company in 2000
notes: Corporate lineage: Meridian Data → Quantum → Snap Appliance private (2002).

metric_value: Spun out of Quantum in 2002 as private company; original IPO plans shelved
notes: Notable corporate event — IPO failure followed by successful private restart.

metric_value: Private company with strong product line and 130k+ installed base; positioned for growth in NAS market
notes: Medium confidence at time of study. Outcome: acquired by Overland Storage 2008, then Sphere 3D 2014. Did not survive independently.

metric_name: Snap Appliance corporate fate — actual outcome
metric_value: Acquired by Overland Storage in 2008; Overland later merged with Sphere 3D in 2014. Company did not survive as independent entity.
notes: Confirmed per prescience context. NAS category thrived; Snap company did not.

metric_value: Sells through manufacturers, distributors, and resellers in North America, Europe, and Asia Pacific
notes: Global distribution reach despite private company status.

metric_name: ATA disk cost advantage for NAS
metric_value: Snap storage appliances use inexpensive ATA disks in variety of redundancy configurations — key cost advantage
notes: ATA cost advantage is core value proposition; aligned to Aberdeen midline thesis.

metric_name: Aberdeen midline categories covered by Snap
metric_value: Snap creatively covered all mid-line storage categories envisaged by Aberdeen
notes: Direct validation of Aberdeen's midline taxonomy by a vendor's product line. Kastner writing this profile validates alignment.

metric_name: Midline use cases served by Snap
metric_value: (1) SCSI-to-tape staging, (2) fixed content long-term storage, (3) light/moderate applications with storage consolidation, (4) data replication, (5) PC client backup
notes: Five-category midline use case taxonomy per Aberdeen framework. Comprehensive coverage claimed.

metric_name: IT professional demand drivers for NAS
metric_value: IT professionals increasingly vocal about urgent need: keep up with storage growth, low-cost alternatives, simple products
notes: Demand-side characterization consistent with broader market trends at time.

metric_name: Primary target market for Snap Appliance
metric_value: SMB companies and enterprises with minimal or no IT staffing — heterogeneous environments
notes: Simple management as key differentiator for non-specialist IT buyers.

metric_value: World's best selling NAS server as of 2003; capable and portable file server
notes: Core workgroup product; validated desktop-to-rack NAS form factor.

metric_value: Snap Server 2200: larger storage capacity with RAID 1 data protection
notes: Entry-level RAID protection in workgroup NAS segment.

metric_value: Snap Server 4100: entry-level RAID 5 in slim rack-mountable chassis
notes: First RAID 5 product in Snap's departmental lineup.

metric_value: Built around 4 ATA-100 disks; 320 GB to 1 TB total; dual gigabit-Ethernet; 1U rack; expandable to 3 TB with Snap Disk 10 arrays
notes: Key departmental product specs. 3 TB maximum with expansion — significant for 2003.

metric_value: 1.44 TB to 3.0 TB across 12 disks in 3U rack; ADS and NIS security protocol support for Unix/Windows datacenters
notes: Enterprise-class product demonstrating Snap's reach beyond SMB into full enterprise NAS.

metric_value: Linux-based OS for departmental and enterprise Snap Servers; snapshots and Windows ADS support; same ease of use as workgroup SnapOS
notes: Snapshots feature in 2003 — early adoption of point-in-time copy in midline NAS.

metric_value: Unlimited user license of DataKeeper desktop backup software for Windows clients included with every Snap Server
notes: Software bundling as value differentiator — consistent with later Dell bundling strategy.

metric_name: NAS as winning midline + ATA combination
metric_value: Snap's NAS + ATA product line validated as winning combination for midline storage tier
notes: Proved correct — NAS as a category thrived enormously. NAS became ubiquitous in enterprise as predicted.

metric_value: NAS became ubiquitous in enterprise storage by 2008-2012 exactly as predicted. Every major vendor built out NAS product lines.
notes: NAS ubiquity confirmed. The category prediction was correct even if Snap the company did not survive independently.

metric_value: Founded 1994 per company facts sidebar
notes: Company age context: 9 years old at time of profile.

metric_value: Aberdeen research cited: Mid-Line Disk Storage Emerging As Significant Cost-Saving Opportunity (October 2003)
notes: Direct cross-reference to Aberdeen's own midline research published same month as this profile.

metric_value: Software partners: APC, Computer Associates, Legato, PowerQuest, SyncSort, Veritas, Sun Microsystems
notes: Diverse software ecosystem supporting Snap's heterogeneous environment claim.

metric_value: Aberdeen framework positions midline ATA/NAS as significant cost-saving opportunity vs FC/SCSI for non-OLTP workloads
notes: This profile is a vendor-specific validation of Aberdeen's October 2003 midline report thesis. The thesis proved correct — SATA/midline became 85% of enterprise data by 2011.

metric_value: Snap Server 4500 with two Snap Disk 10 expansion arrays: up to 3TB of raw storage capacity
notes: 3 TB maximum was a notable capacity at time for departmental NAS.

metric_value: Wide variety of comprehensive service programs for all Snap Servers covering support needs
notes: Service offering cited without specifics — typical vendor profile completeness element.
